title:
5009. Design a Real-Time Error Log Monitoring System
High-Level Design (HLD)System DesignStreaming DataKafkaScalabilityM

transcript.text:
5009. Design a Real-Time Error Log Monitoring System
High-Level Design (HLD)System DesignStreaming DataKafkaScalabilityMonitoring
Hard
Design a system that can ingest and process over 1 million error logs per second from various distributed services. The system must provide sub-second alerting based on specific error patterns or thresholds and allow developers to query the logs for debugging purposes.

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time — ingest, alert, query — then go deep on the two or three hardest parts: how we absorb a million-a-second firehose without dropping logs, how we get sub-second alerts, and how we make months of logs queryable cheaply. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: this is a **write-heavy firehose** (1M+ logs/sec, mostly never read) that must *simultaneously* serve **sub-second alerting** (latency-critical) **and** **ad-hoc debugging queries** (flexible, slower) — three workloads with opposite optimization points pulling on one pipe.

🏢 I'll target a **Google-style bar** (deep on one streaming/distributed problem) but this is squarely an **Uber-flavored** problem too (real-time, high-throughput, ops-first) — tell me if you want me to tilt toward cost/ops (Amazon) or MVP-simplicity (startup) and I'll re-emphasize.

**Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Ingest** — services should be able to emit structured error logs at massive volume without blocking their own request path (fire-and-forget).
- **Alert** — the system should evaluate logs against rules (pattern match, or "more than N errors of type X in window W") and fire a notification in **sub-second** time.
- **Query** — developers should be able to search/filter logs by service, time range, severity, trace-id, and text for debugging.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** log *collection agent* internals on each host, full-text relevance ranking, dashboards/visualization UI, metrics/tracing (we're logs-only), user management/RBAC depth, log *sampling* policy tuning.

**Non-Functional:**
- **High write availability** — ingest path is ~**99.99%** ("4 nines"); dropping a customer's request because *our logging* is down is unacceptable. Query path can be ~99.9%.
- **Sub-second alerting** — **p99 < 1s** from log emission to alert fired (the headline SLO).
- **Ingest durability without head-of-line blocking** — we accept **at-least-once** delivery (dups OK, loss bad); a slow consumer must never stall producers.
- **Scale** — sustain **1M logs/sec** steady, burst to ~3-5M/sec during incidents (exactly when logs spike *and* alerting matters most).
- **Query latency** — interactive p95 **< 2-3s** over recent (hot) data; minutes is fine for cold/archived data.
- **Consistency stance** — ingest & query are **eventually consistent** (a log may be queryable a second or two after emission); alerting is **time-bounded** not strongly consistent.
- **Retention/cost** — hot searchable ~7 days, cold/archive ~30-90 days cheaply; cost must not scale linearly with the firehose.

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** with at-least-once delivery, alert *counts* can over-count on retries — I'll need idempotent/deduped counting in the alerting deep dive, or accept a bounded error in thresholds. I'll revisit this in the ledger.

> 💬 "The thing I want to anchor on: this is overwhelmingly a write problem — a million a second in, and the vast majority of those logs are never read by a human. So I'll design the ingest path to be dumb, fast, and unkillable, and treat alerting and query as two different read-shaped consumers hanging off the same durable stream."

🎙️ **Script:** "Let me frame the requirements. Functionally there are three things: ingest logs at huge volume, evaluate them for alerts in under a second, and let developers query them for debugging. The hard part is that these three pull in opposite directions — ingest wants to be a dumb fast firehose, alerting is latency-critical and needs windowed state, and query wants rich flexible indexing. Non-functionally, the headline numbers are a million logs a second steady with multi-million bursts during incidents, sub-second p99 alerting, and four-nines on the ingest path because we can't let our own logging take down a customer's request. I'm explicitly choosing at-least-once delivery — I'd rather have duplicate logs than lost ones — which means I'll have to deal with dedup in the alert counters later."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Questions I'd ask (and why each changes the design):**

| Question | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Are logs **structured** (JSON with fields) or **raw text**? | Structured lets me index fields cheaply; raw forces full-text indexing (much more expensive at this scale). |
| Is **log loss ever acceptable** (e.g., sample under extreme load)? | If yes, I can shed load gracefully under burst; if no, I must over-provision the buffer. Drives the durability budget. |
| Who writes alert rules and how many? **Static** thresholds or **dynamic/ML** anomaly detection? | A few thousand static rules → simple stream evaluation. ML/anomaly → a whole separate scoring pipeline. I'll assume static rules. |
| Query access pattern: mostly **recent** (last hours) or **historical** forensics? | Drives hot/cold tiering. I'll assume 90% queries hit last 24h. |
| Retention & compliance (PII in logs, right-to-erasure)? | Affects storage tier, encryption, and whether I can keep raw payloads. |
| Multi-region — are services global? Do alerts need to be region-local? | Drives whether I run one pipeline per region or aggregate globally. |

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:**
- Logs are **structured JSON**, avg **~1 KB** each (service, level, timestamp, trace_id, message, tags).
- **At-least-once**, no silent loss under steady state; under extreme burst I may **sample low-severity** logs (debug/info) but never errors.
- **~10,000 static alert rules**, evaluated in-stream; sub-second target.
- **90% of queries** target the last 24h; full-text on `message` + structured filters.
- **Multi-region**, pipeline runs **per-region** with alerting region-local; query can fan out cross-region.
- Read:write is wildly write-skewed — essentially **all writes, few reads**.

> 💬 "I'm going to assume structured JSON logs around a kilobyte each, about ten thousand static alerting rules, and that nearly all debugging queries hit the last day of data. If any of those is wrong — especially if you want ML-based anomaly detection instead of static thresholds — tell me, because that adds a whole scoring pipeline."

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size it — are those assumptions reasonable, or do you want to push on any? Specifically, should I plan for ML anomaly detection, or are static threshold/pattern rules enough for today?"

Now let me put a few numbers behind this — I'll defer full estimation and only compute what actually drives a decision.

blocks.name: run_command

input.command:
cat > capacity.py << 'EOF'
# ---- Inputs ----
logs_per_sec = 1_000_000          # steady ingest
burst_factor = 4                  # incident burst -> ~4M/s
avg_log_bytes = 1024              # ~1 KB structured JSON

# ---- Ingest throughput ----
steady_bps = logs_per_sec * avg_log_bytes          # bytes/sec
burst_lps = logs_per_sec * burst_factor

print("=== Ingest ===")
print(f"Steady: {logs_per_sec:,} logs/s")
print(f"Burst : {burst_lps:,} logs/s")
print(f"Steady ingest bandwidth: {steady_bps/1e9:.2f} GB/s  ({steady_bps*8/1e9:.1f} Gbit/s)")
print(f"Burst  ingest bandwidth: {burst_lps*avg_log_bytes/1e9:.2f} GB/s")

# ---- Per-day / storage ----
secs_day = 86400
logs_day = logs_per_sec * secs_day
raw_day_bytes = logs_day * avg_log_bytes
print("\n=== Volume / Storage ===")
print(f"Logs/day: {logs_day/1e9:.1f} billion")
print(f"Raw/day : {raw_day_bytes/1e12:.1f} TB/day")

# hot tier (7 days) on indexed store, assume ~1.5x for index, 0.3x compression on payload
hot_days = 7
index_mult = 1.5      # inverted index + doc store overhead
compress = 0.35       # JSON compresses well
hot_bytes = raw_day_bytes * hot_days * index_mult * compress
print(f"Hot tier (7d, indexed, compressed): {hot_bytes/1e12:.0f} TB")

# cold archive (90 days) object storage, heavy compression, no index
cold_days = 90
cold_compress = 0.15
cold_bytes = raw_day_bytes * cold_days * cold_compress
print(f"Cold archive (90d, compressed, no index): {cold_bytes/1e15:.2f} PB")

# ---- Kafka partition sizing ----
part_throughput = 50_000   # logs/s a single partition+consumer comfortably handles
parts_steady = logs_per_sec / part_throughput
parts_burst  = burst_lps / part_throughput
print("\n=== Kafka partitions ===")
print(f"Partitions for steady: {parts_steady:.0f}")
print(f"Partitions for burst : {parts_burst:.0f}")

# ---- Alerting state ----
rules = 10_000
print("\n=== Alerting ===")
print(f"Rules evaluated per log: {rules:,} (need pre-indexed rule matching, not 10k scans/log)")
print(f"Naive cost: {logs_per_sec*rules/1e9:.0f} billion rule-checks/s  <-- infeasible, must index rules")

# ---- Buffer to absorb consumer outage ----
buffer_minutes = 30
buffer_bytes = steady_bps * 60 * buffer_minutes
print("\n=== Durability buffer ===")
print(f"30-min replay buffer at steady: {buffer_bytes/1e12:.1f} TB (x replication factor 3 = {3*buffer_bytes/1e12:.1f} TB)")

# ---- Monthly cost rough ----
print("\n=== Rough monthly cost drivers ===")
hot_cost = hot_bytes/1e12 * 1500   # ~$ / TB-month for hot indexed (SSD + compute), rough
cold_cost = cold_bytes/1e12 * 20   # ~$20/TB-month object storage
print(f"Hot search tier  : ~${hot_cost/1000:.0f}K/mo")
print(f"Cold archive     : ~${cold_cost/1000:.0f}K/mo")
EOF
python3 capacity.py

blocks.output:
=== Ingest ===
Steady: 1,000,000 logs/s
Burst : 4,000,000 logs/s
Steady ingest bandwidth: 1.02 GB/s  (8.2 Gbit/s)
Burst  ingest bandwidth: 4.10 GB/s

=== Volume / Storage ===
Logs/day: 86.4 billion
Raw/day : 88.5 TB/day
Hot tier (7d, indexed, compressed): 325 TB
Cold archive (90d, compressed, no index): 1.19 PB

=== Kafka partitions ===
Partitions for steady: 20
Partitions for burst : 80

=== Alerting ===
Rules evaluated per log: 10,000 (need pre-indexed rule matching, not 10k scans/log)
Naive cost: 10 billion rule-checks/s  <-- infeasible, must index rules

=== Durability buffer ===
30-min replay buffer at steady: 1.8 TB (x replication factor 3 = 5.5 TB)

=== Rough monthly cost drivers ===
Hot search tier  : ~$488K/mo
Cold archive     : ~$24K/mo

blocks.md:
## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'm deferring full estimation — here are only the numbers that actually force a design decision:

| Metric | Value | Rounded / say-out-loud |
|---|---|---|
| Steady ingest | 1M logs/s | "a million a second" |
| Burst (incident) | 4M logs/s | "spikes to four million" |
| Ingest bandwidth | 1.02 GB/s steady | "about a gigabyte a second, ~8 Gbit" |
| Raw volume | 88.5 TB/day | "~90 terabytes a day" |
| Hot tier (7d, indexed) | 325 TB | "a few hundred terabytes hot" |
| Cold archive (90d) | 1.19 PB | "north of a petabyte cold" |
| Kafka partitions | 20 steady / 80 burst | "tens of partitions" |
| Naive rule eval | 10 **billion** checks/s | "infeasible — must index rules" |
| Hot search cost | ~$488K/mo | "the dominant cost driver" |

**QPS** *(queries/queries-per-second — here it's the write rate, "log-events per second")* on the write side is the whole game: ~1M/s.

**The ONE number that forces the design:** the naive alerting cost — **1M logs/s × 10K rules = 10 billion rule-checks/second** — is wildly infeasible. That single number kills the obvious "loop every rule over every log" design and forces me to **invert the problem: index the rules and match each log against only the few that could apply** (deep dive §8). The **flip threshold:** below ~a few hundred rules you could brute-force per log; above that you must pre-index rules by (service, level, pattern).

Secondary forcing number: **hot search storage at ~$488K/mo dominates cost** — so I'll aggressively tier hot→cold and never full-text-index the cold petabyte.

🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything: if I naively check ten thousand rules against a million logs a second, that's ten billion comparisons a second — completely infeasible. So the whole alerting design flips to indexing the *rules*, not the logs. On volume, it's about ninety terabytes a day raw, which is a few hundred terabytes hot and over a petabyte cold — storage isn't a throughput problem, it's a *cost* problem, and the hot search tier at roughly half a million a month is my dominant cost driver. Partition-wise it's only tens of Kafka partitions, so the firehose itself is very tractable — the buffer to survive a 30-minute consumer outage is under 2 terabytes."

## 4. Core Entities

A first draft of the key nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model (§7):

- **Log Event** — a single structured error record (service, level, timestamp, trace_id, message, tags). The atomic unit flowing through the whole system.
- **Service / Source** — the producer that emitted the log; logs are partitioned and queried by it.
- **Alert Rule** — a developer-defined condition: a pattern match or a threshold over a time window for a (service, level, pattern).
- **Alert (firing)** — an instance of a rule tripping, with dedup state so we don't spam.
- **Window Counter** — the running aggregate (count of matching events per rule per time bucket) that powers threshold alerts.
- **Subscription / Channel** — where a fired alert goes (PagerDuty, Slack, email).

🎙️ **Script:** "The core nouns are simple: a Log Event is the atomic record flowing through everything; an Alert Rule is a developer-defined condition; an Alert is an instance of a rule firing; and a Window Counter is the running aggregate that lets me do 'more than N errors in W seconds' without rescanning. That's the first draft — I'll nail down the exact fields when I get to the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Going one-by-one through the functional requirements:

**Ingest** — high-throughput, fire-and-forget:
```
POST /v1/logs:batch
Body: { "logs": [ {service, level, ts, trace_id, message, tags{}}, ... ] }   // batched, gzipped
Resp: 202 Accepted { "received": N }    // async; we ACK receipt, NOT processing
```
Why: **POST** (creates events), **batched** (a per-log HTTP call at 1M/s is absurd — agents buffer ~1000 logs/flush), **202 not 200** (we've durably buffered, not processed). The caller is a trusted internal **collector agent** authed by mTLS/service token — we identify the service from the token, **never trust a client-supplied service name blindly** for authz (but accept it as a label).

**Alert** — rule management (low QPS, strong consistency):
```
POST   /v1/rules         {service, level, pattern, threshold, window_s, channel}  -> 201
GET    /v1/rules/{id}                                                              -> 200
PUT    /v1/rules/{id}     {…}    // versioned update                               -> 200
DELETE /v1/rules/{id}                                                              -> 204
```
Why standard REST CRUD: rules are a small, consistent control-plane dataset. PUT is **idempotent** for safe retries.

**Query** — developer debugging:
```
GET /v1/logs?service=&level=&from=&to=&trace_id=&q=text&cursor=&limit=
Resp: 200 { results:[…], next_cursor }   // cursor pagination, not offset
```
Why: **GET** (read), **time-range required** (forces a bounded scan, protects the cluster), **cursor pagination** (offset pagination over billions of rows is death), `q` is full-text on `message`.

> 💬 "Two deliberate API choices I'd call out: ingest is batched and returns 202 — I'm acknowledging that I've safely buffered the logs, not that I've processed them, which is what lets the producer fire-and-forget. And query *forces* a time range so nobody can accidentally scan the whole petabyte."

🎙️ **Script:** "On the API: ingest is a batched POST that returns 202 Accepted — agents on each host buffer a thousand logs and flush, and we ACK that we've durably buffered, not processed, so producers never block. Rules are plain REST CRUD because that's a small strongly-consistent control plane. And query is a GET that *requires* a time range and uses cursor pagination — both are guardrails so a careless query can't scan billions of rows. Everything's authed by service token; I treat the client's service name as a label, not as authorization."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Ingest logs at massive volume without blocking producers"

The foundational slice: get a million logs a second into a durable buffer that decouples producers from every downstream consumer.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Collector Agent | Fluent Bit / Vector (sidecar) | Buffers + batches on each host, retries locally | Host-local loss if host dies pre-flush |
| Ingest Gateway | Stateless service behind L4 LB | Auth, validate, append to log; scales horizontally | Just a thin pass-through (intentional) |
| Message Log | **Kafka** | Durable, ordered, replayable buffer; decouples producers from consumers; survives slow consumers | Operational complexity |

> **Kafka** *(a durable, replicated, append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow or down — here it's the shock absorber between producers and every downstream reader)*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph Clients
    SVC[Services + Collector agent]:::client
  end
  GW[Ingest Gateway]:::svc
  K[[Kafka log topic]]:::async
  SVC -->|1 POST batch mTLS| GW
  GW -->|2 validate + append| K
  GW -.->|3 202 Accepted| SVC
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```
↳ reuses existing: none (this is the foundation).

1. Service emits a log; the **collector agent** batches ~1000 and POSTs over mTLS.
2. **Ingest Gateway** authenticates the service token, does cheap validation (size, schema), and appends the batch to **Kafka**, partitioned by `service` (or `hash(trace_id)` to keep a trace together). *State change:* logs are now durably persisted (replication factor 3) and replayable.
3. Gateway returns **202** immediately — producer is unblocked. Downstream consumers read at their own pace.

> 💬 "I draw the Kafka log first and say: everything hangs off this. The gateway's only job is to authenticate and append — it's deliberately dumb so it can scale to a million a second and basically never fail."

🎙️ **Script:** "I'll start with the ingest backbone. Collector agents on each host batch logs and POST them to a stateless ingest gateway, which authenticates, does cheap validation, and appends to Kafka — a durable replicated log that's the shock absorber for the whole system. I partition by service so one noisy service's logs stay ordered together. The gateway returns 202 the instant the batch is in Kafka, so producers fire-and-forget. The key idea: Kafka decouples the million-a-second firehose from everyone downstream, so a slow consumer or a crashed query cluster can never push back on the services emitting logs."

### 6.2 "Evaluate logs against rules and fire sub-second alerts"

Now hang a **stream processor** off Kafka that maintains windowed counters and fires alerts — without rescanning all 10K rules per log.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stream Processor | **Flink** | Stateful windowed aggregation, exactly-once state, sub-second latency | Heavy to operate, stateful |
| Rule Index | In-memory (broadcast state) | Match each log to only applicable rules via (service, level) index | Must propagate rule updates |
| Counter State | Flink RocksDB state / **Redis** | Fast read-modify-write of window counts | State size grows with rules×windows |
| Alert Dispatcher | Service → PagerDuty/Slack | Dedup + route fired alerts | External dependency latency |

> **Flink** *(a stream-processing engine that keeps running state — like windowed counters — in memory with checkpointing, so it can do "count errors in the last 10 seconds" continuously instead of re-querying a database — here it's the sub-second alerting brain)*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  K[[Kafka log topic]]:::async
  F[Flink alert processor]:::svc
  RI[(Rule index broadcast)]:::store
  CS[(Window counters Redis-RocksDB)]:::store
  AD[Alert Dispatcher]:::svc
  EXT[PagerDuty Slack]:::ext
  K -->|1 consume| F
  RI -.->|2 matching rules| F
  F -->|3 incr window count| CS
  F -->|4 threshold tripped| AD
  AD -->|5 deduped notify| EXT
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
  classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe;
```
↳ reuses existing: **Kafka log topic** (from §6.1).

1. **Flink** consumes the Kafka topic in parallel (one subtask per partition).
2. For each log, it looks up the **rule index** (broadcast state keyed by `service`+`level`) to get *only* the handful of rules that could match — not all 10K. Pattern rules fire immediately; threshold rules continue.
3. It increments the **window counter** for each matching threshold rule (e.g., sliding 10s window). *State change:* per-rule count updated.
4. If a count crosses its threshold, emit a candidate alert to the **dispatcher**.
5. Dispatcher **dedups** (one alert per rule per cooldown) and routes to PagerDuty/Slack. *State change:* alert marked firing.

> 💬 "The trick I say out loud: I never loop 10,000 rules per log. I index the rules by service and level, so each log only touches the two or three rules that could possibly apply. That's what turns ten billion checks a second into something tractable."

🎙️ **Script:** "For alerting I attach a Flink job to the same Kafka topic. Flink is a stream processor that keeps windowed state in memory with checkpointing, so it can continuously answer 'how many errors of this type in the last ten seconds' without hammering a database. The critical move is rule indexing: instead of testing every log against ten thousand rules, I broadcast a rule index keyed by service and level, so each log only matches the two or three rules that could apply. Pattern rules fire instantly; threshold rules bump a sliding-window counter, and when it crosses the limit I emit to a dispatcher that dedups and routes to PagerDuty. End to end that's well under a second because nothing touches disk on the hot path."

### 6.3 "Query logs for debugging"

A second consumer off Kafka indexes logs into a searchable store and tiers old data to cheap archive.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indexer | Kafka consumer group | Bulk-writes logs into search store | Lag = query staleness |
| Hot Search Store | **OpenSearch/Elasticsearch** | Inverted index for full-text + structured filters on recent data | Expensive; can't hold a petabyte |
| Cold Archive | **S3** (Parquet) | Cheap, durable long-term; queried via Athena/Presto | Slow (seconds-minutes) queries |
| Query Service | Stateless API | Routes hot vs cold, merges results | Cross-tier merge complexity |

> **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch** *(a search engine built on an inverted index — a map from each word to the logs containing it — so full-text and filtered search is fast; here it serves interactive debugging over recent logs)*. **Parquet on S3** *(columnar files in cheap object storage — here the cold archive we scan with Athena when someone needs old forensics)*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  K[[Kafka log topic]]:::async
  IDX[Indexer consumer]:::svc
  ES[(OpenSearch hot 7d)]:::store
  S3[(S3 Parquet cold 90d)]:::store
  QS[Query Service]:::svc
  DEV[Developer]:::client
  K -->|1 consume| IDX
  IDX -->|2 bulk index| ES
  IDX -->|3 batch write| S3
  DEV -->|4 search| QS
  QS -->|5a recent| ES
  QS -->|5b historical| S3
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```
↳ reuses existing: **Kafka log topic** (from §6.1).

1. A separate **indexer** consumer group reads the same Kafka topic (independent offset from Flink).
2. It bulk-indexes recent logs into **OpenSearch** (hot, ~7 days). *State change:* logs become searchable (~1-2s after emission).
3. In parallel it batches logs into **Parquet on S3** for cheap long-term archive.
4. A developer hits the **Query Service** with a time range + filters.
5. Query Service routes: recent → OpenSearch (5a); historical → S3/Athena (5b), merging if the range spans both.

> 💬 "I deliberately run a *separate* consumer group for indexing so the query pipeline and the alerting pipeline can fail independently — if Elasticsearch falls over, alerting keeps firing."

🎙️ **Script:** "Query is a third independent consumer off Kafka. An indexer bulk-writes recent logs into OpenSearch — a search engine with an inverted index for fast full-text and filtered search — and simultaneously batches everything into Parquet files on S3 for cheap long-term storage. The query service routes by time range: recent goes to OpenSearch for sub-second interactive search, historical goes to S3 via Athena where seconds-to-minutes is fine. Because indexing is its own consumer group with its own Kafka offset, the query path and the alert path are fully isolated — if the search cluster dies, alerting doesn't even notice."

### Final (high-level)

The reconciled union of all three slices — nothing dropped, nothing new:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Clients
    SVC[Services + Collector agent]:::client
    DEV[Developer]:::client
  end
  GW[Ingest Gateway]:::svc
  K[[Kafka log topic part by service]]:::async
  subgraph Alerting hot path
    F[Flink alert processor]:::svc
    RI[(Rule index)]:::store
    CS[(Window counters Redis)]:::store
    AD[Alert Dispatcher]:::svc
    EXT[PagerDuty Slack]:::ext
  end
  subgraph Query path
    IDX[Indexer consumer]:::svc
    ES[(OpenSearch hot 7d msg trace_id ts)]:::store
    S3[(S3 Parquet cold 90d)]:::store
    QS[Query Service]:::svc
  end
  RDB[(Rules DB Postgres)]:::store
  SVC -->|POST batch| GW --> K
  K --> F
  RI -.-> F
  RDB -.->|load rules| RI
  F --> CS
  F --> AD --> EXT
  K --> IDX
  IDX --> ES
  IDX --> S3
  DEV --> QS
  QS --> ES
  QS --> S3
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
  classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe;
```

🎙️ **Script:** "Putting it together: services push batched logs through a thin ingest gateway into Kafka, partitioned by service. Kafka fans out to three independent consumers. Flink does sub-second windowed alerting against an indexed rule set, keeping counters in Redis and firing through a deduping dispatcher to PagerDuty. An indexer writes recent logs to OpenSearch for interactive search and archives everything to Parquet on S3. The query service routes hot versus cold. The whole design rests on one idea — Kafka decouples the firehose from three consumers that scale, fail, and evolve independently."

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The three areas I think are richest are: surviving the burst without dropping logs, hitting sub-second alerting with the rule-explosion problem, and keeping query cheap over a petabyte. Which do you want me to go deep on first?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

| Entity | Key fields (type — note) | Store | Partition/Shard key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Log Event** | id (uuid), service (str), level (enum), ts (int64), trace_id (str), message (text), tags (map) | Kafka → OpenSearch (hot) + S3 Parquet (cold) | `service` (Kafka), time-based index (ES) | Eventual (~1-2s) |
| **Alert Rule** | id, service, level, pattern (regex), threshold (int), window_s (int), channel, version | **PostgreSQL** | id | Strong |
| **Window Counter** | rule_id + window_bucket → count | **Redis** / Flink RocksDB state | rule_id | Strong-ish within processor |
| **Alert (firing)** | id, rule_id, fired_at, dedup_key, status | PostgreSQL | rule_id | Strong |

Storage reasoning, one line each:
- **Kafka** for the raw stream: it's the durable, replayable buffer — the only thing that must absorb 1M/s without backpressure.
- **OpenSearch** for hot query: inverted index is the *only* way to make ad-hoc full-text + filtered debugging fast; we cap it at ~7 days because indexing a petabyte is uneconomical.
- **S3 Parquet** for cold: columnar + cheap object storage; scanned by Athena only when someone needs old forensics — access pattern is rare and latency-tolerant.
- **PostgreSQL** for rules/alerts: small, strongly-consistent control-plane data where ACID matters (a half-written rule is dangerous).
- **Redis** for counters: ephemeral, blazing-fast read-modify-write with TTL auto-expiry on old windows — durability isn't critical because Flink can rebuild from Kafka replay.

🎙️ **Script:** "On storage, I match each store to its access pattern. Kafka holds the raw stream because it's the one thing that must absorb a million a second durably. OpenSearch holds the last seven days because an inverted index is the only way to make ad-hoc debugging fast — but I refuse to index the cold petabyte, so older logs go to Parquet on S3, scanned by Athena only when someone actually needs forensics. Rules live in Postgres because they're small and need ACID — a half-written rule could mis-fire alerts. And the window counters live in Redis for fast increments with TTL expiry; I don't need them durable because Flink can rebuild state from Kafka replay."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "the firehose is the constraint" — every hard question here is some variation of *how do I avoid touching disk, or scanning everything, on the 1M/s path.*

### How do we evaluate 10K rules against 1M logs/sec for sub-second alerts?

#### Bad: scan every rule per log
**Approach:** for each incoming log, loop all 10,000 rules and test each. **Challenges:** 1M × 10K = **10 billion checks/sec** — infeasible on any cluster. ⚠️ **Trap:** candidates describe "a service that evaluates the rules" without doing this multiplication and walk straight into a 10-billion-ops wall.

#### Good: index rules; stateful windowing in Flink
**Approach:** build a **rule index** keyed by `(service, level)` and broadcast it to all Flink subtasks. Each log looks up only the handful of rules for its service+level (usually <10). Threshold rules use Flink **sliding-window** state. **Challenges:** rule updates must propagate to all subtasks (broadcast stream handles this); hot services with many rules can still be heavy; window state grows with rules × buckets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  K[[Kafka log topic]]:::async
  F[Flink keyed by service]:::svc
  RI[(Rule index broadcast)]:::store
  W[(Sliding window state)]:::store
  K -->|log| F
  RI -.->|matching rules only| F
  F -->|incr| W
  F -->|trip| AD[Dispatcher]:::svc
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```

#### Great: indexed rules + tumbling-window pre-aggregation + at-least-once dedup
**Approach:** partition the stream by `(service, level)` so each key's rules and counters live on one subtask (no cross-shard coordination). Use **tumbling sub-windows** (e.g., 1s buckets) pre-aggregated, then sum buckets for the W-second sliding window — turns per-log work into per-bucket work. Because delivery is **at-least-once**, dedup counts using `(log_id)` within the processor (Flink exactly-once checkpointing on the *counter state*) so a Kafka replay after a crash doesn't double-count and false-fire. **Trade-off:** exactly-once checkpointing adds latency (~hundreds of ms at checkpoint) and operational weight, but it's what keeps thresholds accurate. This is what real systems (e.g., streaming SIEM/observability pipelines) actually do.

🎙️ **Script:** "The naive answer — loop every rule per log — is ten billion checks a second, dead on arrival. So I invert it: I index the rules by service and level and broadcast that index into Flink, so each log only touches the two or three rules that could match. Threshold rules use one-second tumbling buckets that I sum into the sliding window, which turns per-log work into cheap per-bucket work. And because I chose at-least-once delivery, I lean on Flink's exactly-once *state* checkpointing and dedup by log id, so a crash-and-replay doesn't double-count and false-fire an alert."

🧠 **If they ask "why Flink not just Redis counters?":** "I could increment Redis counters from a plain consumer, and for pure counting that works. But Flink gives me exactly-once state, event-time windows that handle late/out-of-order logs, and checkpointed recovery — without it, a consumer crash either loses counts or double-counts on replay. Redis is my fallback for simpler deployments."

### How do we survive the 4M/sec incident burst without dropping error logs?

#### Good: over-provision Kafka + autoscale consumers
**Approach:** size Kafka partitions for burst (~80), let it absorb the spike on disk (it's just sequential append), and **autoscale** Flink/indexer consumers. Kafka's 30-min replay buffer (~1.8 TB, ~5.5 TB at RF3) means consumers can lag and catch up. **Challenges:** autoscaling isn't instant; during the spike, consumer lag grows and alerting latency can blow past 1s exactly when it matters most.

#### Great: priority lanes + load-shedding by severity
**Approach:** split into **priority topics** — `errors`/`fatal` go to a high-priority topic that's never sampled; `debug`/`info` go to a separate topic that the gateway **sheds (samples)** under backpressure. Alerting only consumes the error lane, so its latency is protected from the debug flood. Keep alerting consumers **statically provisioned for peak** (cheap relative to the cost of missing an alert) rather than relying on reactive autoscaling. **Trade-off:** we lose some low-severity logs during incidents — acceptable, since we explicitly said errors are sacred and debug is not.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  GW[Ingest Gateway]:::svc
  KE[[Kafka errors lane]]:::async
  KD[[Kafka debug lane sheddable]]:::async
  GW -->|errors never dropped| KE
  GW -->|debug shed under load| KD
  KE --> F[Flink alerting peak-sized]:::svc
  KE --> IDX[Indexer]:::svc
  KD --> IDX
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
```

🎙️ **Script:** "Bursts are dangerous because logs spike exactly when alerting matters most. Kafka absorbs the raw spike fine — it's sequential disk append — but consumer lag is the risk. So I split into priority lanes: errors and fatals go to a lane that's never sampled and feeds alerting, which I statically provision for peak rather than hoping autoscaling reacts in time. Debug and info go to a separate lane the gateway sheds under backpressure. I'm explicitly trading away low-severity logs during an incident to guarantee error logs and sub-second alerting survive."

🧠 **If they ask "isn't dropping logs bad?":** "Only low-severity ones, and only under extreme load — I never drop errors. The alternative, over-provisioning everything for a 4x burst that happens rarely, wastes money 99% of the time. Load-shedding by severity is the standard observability trade-off."

### How do we keep query fast and cheap over a petabyte?

#### Bad: index everything in Elasticsearch
**Approach:** put all 90 days in OpenSearch. **Challenges:** indexing a petabyte costs millions/month and the cluster becomes unstable; ⚠️ **Trap:** "just use Elasticsearch for everything" ignores that the inverted index is ~the size of the data and storage cost dominates.

#### Great: hot/cold tiering + time-based indices + rollover
**Approach:** OpenSearch holds **~7 days** in time-based indices (one per day) so old indices are dropped cheaply with `rollover`. Everything goes to **S3 Parquet** (columnar, ~85% compression) queried by **Athena/Presto** for the rare historical query. Query service routes by `from`/`to`. Force time-range on queries so no unbounded scans. **Trade-off:** historical queries are seconds-to-minutes, not sub-second — fine, since 90% of debugging hits the last 24h.

🎙️ **Script:** "Indexing the whole petabyte in Elasticsearch would cost millions a month because the inverted index is roughly the size of the data. So I tier: seven days hot in OpenSearch using daily time-based indices that I can drop instantly with rollover, and everything archived to compressed Parquet on S3, queried by Athena only when someone needs old forensics. The query API forces a time range and routes hot versus cold. I'm trading slow historical queries for an order-of-magnitude cost cut, which is fine because ninety percent of debugging is the last day."

🧠 **If they ask "what about a global search across all 90 days?":** "It hits both tiers — OpenSearch for recent, Athena over S3 for the rest, merged by the query service. It's slower, but it's a rare forensic operation, so I optimize the common recent case and accept latency on the long tail."

### How do we keep a log's trace together and avoid hot partitions?

#### Good → Great: partition by service, sub-key by trace
**Approach:** partition Kafka by `service` for locality, but a single huge service (e.g., the API gateway) becomes a **hot partition**. **Great:** use a **composite key** `hash(service + trace_id) % N` so one service spreads across partitions while a given trace stays on one partition (ordered together for debugging). For alerting that needs per-service counts, Flink re-keys by `service` after consumption. **Trade-off:** re-keying adds a shuffle, but it decouples ingest balance from alerting grouping.

🎙️ **Script:** "If I naively partition Kafka by service, one giant service creates a hot partition. So I partition by a hash of service plus trace-id — that spreads a busy service across partitions while keeping each trace on one partition so it stays ordered for debugging. Flink then re-keys by service for per-service alert counts. The cost is a shuffle, but it cleanly separates ingest balance from alerting grouping."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**Availability:** ingest path **99.99%** (stateless gateway + multi-AZ Kafka RF3); query path **99.9%**.

**Per-dependency graceful degradation:**
- **OpenSearch down** → alerting & ingest unaffected (separate consumer); query returns "recent search degraded," falls back to S3/Athena.
- **Flink down** → alerting pauses; Kafka retains data; on recovery Flink replays from last checkpoint — alerts are *delayed*, not lost (bounded by replay buffer).
- **Redis (counters) down** → Flink rebuilds counter state from Kafka replay / its own RocksDB checkpoint.
- **Kafka partition unavailable** → producers retry to other partitions via gateway; RF3 means a broker loss is transparent.
- **Dispatcher/PagerDuty down** → queue alerts, retry with backoff; dedup prevents a flood when it recovers.

**RPO/RTO per data class:**
- Raw logs (Kafka): RPO ~0 (RF3, synchronous), RTO minutes (broker failover).
- Hot index (OpenSearch): RPO = rebuildable from Kafka within retention; RTO = reindex time. It's a *derived* store — not source of truth.
- Rules (Postgres): RPO seconds (WAL replication), RTO minutes; **tested restore** monthly.
- Cold archive (S3): 11 nines durability, effectively never lost.

**Multi-AZ vs multi-region:** Kafka + consumers span **AZs** within a region (transparent failover). **Multi-region** runs an independent pipeline per region with **region-local alerting**; query fans out cross-region. Failover trigger: region health check + alert SLO burn.

**Cost (rough monthly):** **Hot OpenSearch tier ~$488K/mo is the dominant driver** — far exceeds cold archive (~$24K/mo) and Kafka/compute. The single biggest cost lever is shrinking the hot retention window or sampling debug logs out of the index.

**Observability of the monitor itself:** SLO **burn-rate alerts** on alerting latency (p99) and consumer lag; distributed tracing on the query path; runbooks for "consumer lag climbing" and "OpenSearch red." (Meta-point: who monitors the monitor? A small independent watchdog on a separate pipeline.)

🎙️ **Script:** "Reliability hinges on Kafka being the durable source of truth and everything downstream being a *derived* store I can rebuild. If OpenSearch dies, I lose recent search but alerting and ingest don't notice. If Flink dies, alerts are delayed, not lost — it replays from checkpoint within the buffer window. The only true source-of-truth data, the rules, lives in replicated Postgres with a tested monthly restore. Cost-wise the hot search tier dominates at roughly half a million a month, so my biggest lever is hot retention length. And I monitor the monitor with an independent watchdog — because the worst failure is the alerting system going down silently during the incident it's supposed to catch."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

| Decision | What I gave up | What would reverse it |
|---|---|---|
| **At-least-once + dedup** (not exactly-once end-to-end) | Some complexity in counter dedup; tiny over-count risk if dedup window is exceeded | If thresholds must be *exactly* accurate for billing/SLA penalties, I'd pay for true exactly-once across the whole pipeline (latency cost). |
| **Load-shed debug logs under burst** | Completeness of low-severity logs during incidents | If a compliance/audit requirement says *no log may ever be dropped*, I'd over-provision for 4x peak permanently (~4x ingest cost). |
| **7-day hot / Athena cold** | Fast historical queries (cold is seconds-minutes) | If forensic queries over 90 days became a frequent hot path, I'd extend OpenSearch retention or add a mid-tier (e.g., ClickHouse) — at large cost. |

These tie back to the opening SLOs: I optimized **sub-second alerting** and **four-nines ingest** at the explicit expense of **historical query speed** and **low-severity completeness under burst** — and flagged the at-least-once over-count gap from §1.

🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'm least certain about: choosing at-least-once over exactly-once — fine unless thresholds drive billing penalties; shedding debug logs under burst — fine unless there's a no-drop compliance rule; and seven-day hot retention — fine unless deep forensics becomes a daily need, in which case I'd add a ClickHouse mid-tier. Each is a conscious trade of cost against a requirement I ranked lower, and each flips at a clear, nameable threshold."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**1. Why Kafka and not just a load balancer to your processing services?**
Kafka is a *durable buffer*, not just transport — it decouples producers from consumers so a slow or crashed consumer never backpressures the million-a-second producers, and it lets multiple independent consumers (alerting, indexing) read the same stream at their own offsets. A plain LB+service would drop logs the moment a consumer fell behind, and couldn't replay. The trade-off is operational weight, but at this scale durability and replay are non-negotiable.
> 💬 "Kafka isn't transport, it's a shock absorber with replay — that's what protects producers and lets alerting and query consume independently."

**2. How exactly do you hit sub-second alerting?**
Nothing on the hot path touches disk synchronously: Flink consumes from Kafka in memory, matches each log against an indexed rule subset (not all 10K), increments in-memory windowed counters, and emits on threshold cross. The dominant latency is the checkpoint interval (~hundreds of ms) and dispatcher network hop, summing well under a second. The risk is consumer lag during bursts, which I mitigate with peak-provisioned, error-lane-only alerting consumers.
> 💬 "The whole alert path is in-memory stream processing against indexed rules — the only real latency is checkpointing and the notification hop."

**3. Your alert counts could be wrong with at-least-once — how?**
On a consumer crash, Kafka replays uncommitted offsets, so a log could be counted twice and false-fire a threshold. I fix this with Flink's exactly-once *state* checkpointing plus dedup on `log_id` within the window, so replays are idempotent. The residual risk is a duplicate arriving *after* its window's dedup state expired — bounded and rare, and I'd widen the dedup TTL if a rule is penalty-sensitive.
> 💬 "Replays can double-count, so I dedup by log-id inside the window and rely on Flink's exactly-once state — idempotent counting, not idempotent delivery."

**4. What happens during a 4M/sec incident burst?**
Kafka absorbs the raw spike as sequential disk append with a ~30-min replay buffer. Errors flow on a never-sampled priority lane to peak-provisioned alerting consumers, so alert latency holds. Debug/info logs flow on a separate lane the gateway sheds under backpressure. So I protect the two things that matter — error capture and sub-second alerting — by sacrificing low-severity completeness.
> 💬 "Errors get a protected lane and peak-sized consumers; debug gets shed. The system degrades exactly where it's safe to."

**5. Why not index everything in Elasticsearch for query?**
The inverted index is roughly the size of the raw data, so a petabyte would cost millions a month and destabilize the cluster. I keep ~7 days hot (90% of queries) and archive the rest to compressed Parquet on S3, queried by Athena for rare forensics. The trade is slow historical queries, acceptable given the access pattern.
> 💬 "Indexing a petabyte costs millions — I index the recent 7 days people actually query and archive the rest cheaply."

**6. How do you avoid hot partitions from one massive service?**
Partition by `hash(service + trace_id)` so a busy service spreads across partitions while each trace stays ordered on one. Flink re-keys by service after consumption for per-service alert counts. This decouples ingest balance from alerting grouping at the cost of a shuffle.
> 💬 "Composite hash key spreads the hot service but keeps each trace together; Flink re-keys for counting."

**7. How do rule updates propagate without restarting Flink?**
Rules live in Postgres; a change publishes onto a Kafka **broadcast stream** that Flink merges into its broadcast state, so all subtasks see the new rule within seconds with no restart. Versioning each rule lets in-flight windows finish on the old version cleanly.
> 💬 "Rules ride a broadcast stream into Flink state — live updates, no restart, versioned for in-flight windows."

**8. How is read-your-own-writes handled for query?**
It isn't strong — a log is queryable ~1-2s after emission once the indexer commits to OpenSearch. For debugging that's acceptable. If a developer needs to confirm a *just-now* log, I can expose a "tail" view reading directly from Kafka's head for the last few seconds, bypassing the index.
> 💬 "Query is eventually consistent at a second or two; for 'did my log just land' I'd tail Kafka's head directly."

**9. How do you secure ingest against spoofing and abuse?**
Collector agents auth via mTLS/service tokens; the gateway derives the service identity from the token and treats the client-supplied `service` field as a label, not authorization. Per-service rate limits and quotas prevent one service from flooding the pipeline, and payloads are size-capped.
> 💬 "mTLS identifies the producer; the claimed service name is just a label. Per-service quotas stop floods."

**10. PII and right-to-erasure in logs?**
Logs may contain PII, so I encrypt at rest, restrict query access by RBAC, and set retention TTLs (hot 7d, cold 90d auto-expire). Right-to-erasure is hard on append-only Parquet — I'd partition archives by user/tenant where possible and rewrite affected partitions, or tokenize PII at ingest so erasure is deleting a token mapping. I'd flag this as a real cost.
> 💬 "Encrypt, RBAC, TTL — and for erasure I'd tokenize PII at ingest so deletion is just dropping the token map, since rewriting Parquet is painful."

**11. How do you handle late/out-of-order logs in windows?**
Flink uses **event-time** windowing with a bounded **watermark** (e.g., allow 5s lateness), so a log delayed in the network still lands in its correct time window. Logs later than the watermark go to a side output and can update counts with a correction or are dropped per policy. This keeps threshold windows accurate despite network jitter.
> 💬 "Event-time windows with a few seconds of watermark slack handle late logs; anything later than that is a tracked side-output."

**12. What's the single biggest bottleneck and its ceiling?**
The alerting stream processor under burst — if rule-matching or counter updates can't keep up, consumer lag grows and breaks the sub-second SLO. Its ceiling is per-subtask throughput (~50K logs/s); relief is more partitions/subtasks and the error-only lane, raising the effective ceiling to multi-million/s. Beyond that I'd pre-aggregate at the gateway.
> 💬 "The binding constraint is alerting throughput under burst; I relieve it with more partitions and an error-only lane, and pre-aggregation if we go higher."

**13. How would you extend this to anomaly detection (no static thresholds)?**
I'd add a parallel consumer that computes per-service baselines (rolling mean/stddev or a lightweight model) and scores deviations, feeding the same dispatcher. It runs off the same Kafka stream so it doesn't touch the ingest or static-alert paths. The challenge is state size and avoiding alert noise, which I'd tune with hysteresis.
> 💬 "Anomaly detection is just another Kafka consumer scoring deviations from a rolling baseline, feeding the same dispatcher — no change to ingest."

**14. Why Flink over Kafka Streams or Spark Streaming?**
Flink gives true low-latency event-time processing with exactly-once state and rich windowing, which Spark's micro-batch can't match for sub-second alerting. Kafka Streams is viable and simpler (no separate cluster) but scales less flexibly for heavy stateful workloads. I'd pick Kafka Streams for a smaller deployment and Flink at this scale.
> 💬 "Flink for true sub-second event-time state at scale; Kafka Streams if we were smaller and wanted less ops."

**15. How do you test/deploy changes to the alerting logic safely?**
Rules are versioned and validated before publish; processor code deploys with Flink savepoints (snapshot state, upgrade, restore) so no counts are lost. I'd shadow-run new alert logic against live traffic comparing fire rates before cutover, and keep rollback to the prior savepoint one command away.
> 💬 "Savepoints make Flink upgrades stateful and reversible; I shadow-run new alert logic before cutover."

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a write-heavy firehose — a million logs a second — that has to do three opposite jobs, so I anchor everything on Kafka as a durable replayable buffer. A thin stateless gateway authenticates and appends; Kafka fans out to three independent consumers. Flink does sub-second alerting by indexing ten thousand rules down to the few that match each log and keeping windowed counters in memory — never the naive ten-billion-checks-a-second scan. An indexer writes the last seven days to OpenSearch for fast debugging and archives everything to cheap Parquet on S3 for rare forensics. Under an incident burst I protect errors and alerting with a never-sampled priority lane and shed only debug logs. Everything downstream of Kafka is a derived store I can rebuild, so my failure story is just replay. The dominant cost is the hot search tier, so my biggest lever is hot retention length. The headline: decouple the firehose from three consumers that scale and fail independently."
